---
title: "Pre-Course Reading"
subtitle: "Recommended reading before the workshop begins"
---

::: {.callout-note}
These materials are provided to help participants from both communities build enough background to engage productively from Day 1. You do not need to be an expert in the other field — the goal is familiarity with key concepts and terminology.
:::

---

## For Participants from Survival Analysis

These readings introduce infectious disease modeling concepts that will be used throughout the workshop.

### Introductory

*Papers and chapters will be listed here*

### Key Concepts to Be Familiar With

- The **SIR/SEIRD compartmental model** framework: what the compartments mean, how transitions are modeled
- The **basic reproduction number R~0~** and the **effective reproduction number R~eff~(t)**
- **Herd immunity** and vaccine-induced protection at the population level
- **Reporting delays and underreporting** as features of epidemic surveillance data
- The difference between **individual-level** and **aggregate (population-level)** infectious disease data

---

## For Participants from Infectious Disease Modeling

These readings introduce survival analysis concepts and methods that will be used throughout the workshop.

### Introductory

*Papers and chapters will be listed here*

### Key Concepts to Be Familiar With

- The **hazard function** and its relationship to survival probability
- **Right-censoring** and **interval-censoring**: what they mean, why they arise, and how they are handled
- **Competing risks**: what they are and why ignoring them can bias estimates
- **Multi-state models**: modeling transitions between multiple states (e.g., S → E → I → R, I → Hospitalized → Discharged/Dead)
- **Frailty models**: how unobserved heterogeneity is incorporated into survival models

---

## Cross-disciplinary Reading

These papers directly address the intersection of survival analysis and infectious disease modeling.

### Recommended

*Cross-disciplinary papers will be listed here ahead of the workshop*

---

## Case Study Background

Brief background documents on the two case studies (A and B) will be distributed to participants approximately 4 weeks before the workshop. They will also be posted here.

| Document | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| Case Study A overview | Natural course of the epidemic: data structure, variables, analytical goals |
| Case Study B overview | Vaccine efficacy: data structure, variables, analytical goals |

See the [Case Studies](case-studies.qmd) page for the full description of both datasets.

---

## Questions?

If you have questions about the pre-reading or want to discuss any of the materials before the workshop, please [open an issue](https://github.com/kylieainslie/lorentz_center_workshop/issues) on the workshop GitHub repository.
